content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 7 - Chapter 10 – “P” for Place: Customer value fulfilmentPart 3 – Channel behaviour and organization. Channel design and management decisions.
Chapter 10 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Part 3 – Channel behaviour and organization. Channel design and management decisions. .

Channel behaviour and organisation (1 of 12)
Channel behaviour
Ideally all channel firms should work together smoothly.
Channel conflict is disagreement among marketing channel members on goals, roles and rewards – that is, on who should do what and for what rewards.
Horizontal conflict is conflict between firms at the same channel level.
Vertical conflict is conflict between different levels of the same channel.
Q: Give examples of businesses that may be likely to experience      (a) horizontal conflict and (b) vertical conflict.

Channel behaviour and organisation (2 of 12)
Vertical marketing systems
A conventional marketing channel consists of one or more independent producers, wholesalers and retailers, each a separate business seeking to maximise its own profits, perhaps even at the expense of profits for the system as a whole.
A vertical marketing system (VMS) combines successive stages of production and distribution under single ownership; channel leadership is established through common ownership.
Q: Is channel conflict always a ‘bad’ thing?

Figure 10.5
A conventional marketing channel versus a vertical marketing system

Channel behaviour and organisation (4 of 12)
Vertical marketing systems (cont.)
There are three main types of VMS:
corporate VMS
contractual VMS
administered VMS.
Each type of VMS uses a different means of setting leadership and power in the channel.

Figure 10.6
The main types of vertical marketing systems

Channel behaviour and organisation (6 of 12)
Vertical marketing systems (cont.)
Corporate VMS (Vertical Marketing System)
A corporate VMS is a vertical marketing system that combines successive stages of production and distribution under single ownership; channel leadership is established through common ownership.
Q: Give an example of a company that uses a corporate VMS. What are some advantages and disadvantages of a corporate VMS?

Channel behaviour and organisation (7 of 12)
Vertical marketing systems (cont.)
Contractual VMS
A contractual VMS is a vertical marketing system in which independent firms at different levels of production and distribution join together through contracts.
The franchise organisation is the most common type of contractual VMS. There are three forms of franchise:
manufacturer-sponsored retailer franchise networks
manufacturer-sponsored wholesaler franchise networks
service-firm-sponsored franchise networks.

Channel behaviour and organisation (8 of 12)
Vertical marketing systems (cont.)
Administered VMS
An administered VMS is a vertical marketing system that coordinates successive stages of production and distribution, not through common ownership or contractual ties but through the size and power of one of the parties.

Channel behaviour and organisation (9 of 12)
Horizontal marketing systems
A horizontal marketing system is a channel arrangement in which two or more companies at one level join together to follow a new marketing opportunity.
Companies may join forces with non-competitors or with competitors.
Q: Why might a company choose to join with a competitor in this form of channel arrangement? Can you give some examples?

Channel behaviour and organisation (10 of 12)
Multichannel distribution systems
A multichannel distribution system occurs when a single firm sets up two or more marketing channels to reach one or more marketing segments.
Marketers using such multichannels:
gain sales with each new channel
also risk offending existing channels.

Figure 10.7
Multichannel distribution system

Channel behaviour and organisation (12 of 12)
Changing channel organisation
Changes in channel organisation arise from:
changes in technology
growth of direct marketing
growth of online marketing.
Disintermediation is the removal of marketing channel intermediaries by goods or service producers, or the displacement of traditional resellers by new and different types of intermediaries.
Q: Can you think of some industries where disintermediation has occurred? What opportunities or problems might arise from disintermediation?

Channel design and management decisions (1 of 3)
Marketing channel design involves analysing consumer needs, setting channel objectives, identifying major channel alternatives and evaluating those alternative to design effective marketing channels.
Analysing consumer needs
Designing the marketing channel starts with finding out what target customers want from the channel.
Setting channel objectives
Marketing channel objectives should be stated in terms of targeted levels of customer service.

Channel design and management decisions (2 of 3)
Identifying major channel alternatives
Types of intermediaries
Most firms have several channel member choices, each of which provides benefits and drawbacks.
Number of marketing intermediaries
Companies can use three strategies to determine the number of channel members to use at each level:
intensive distribution
exclusive distribution
selective distribution.

Channel design and management decisions (3 of 3)
Evaluating the major alternatives
Channel alternatives can be evaluated on:
economic criteria
control issues
adaptability criteria.
Managing and motivating channel members
Channel members must be managed and motivated.
Companies can use integrated partnership relationship management (PRM) systems to coordinate their whole-channel marketing efforts.

A: Horizontal conflict is conflict at the same level in the supply chain. For example, disputes regarding sales territories amongst sales agents. Fast food franchisees may conflict with other franchisees regarding pricing and promotions, car dealers may similarly be in conflict with other car dealers for the same make of car regarding pricing and promotion. Vertical conflict: disputes regarding margins typically between manufacturers and wholesalers, and between wholesalers and retailers. Disagreements regarding what functions will be performed by each level in the channel. For example, manufacturers may require more comprehensive customer service and advice be given by their retailers to their end-users. Some manufacturers might be concerned that a particular retailer will heavily discount their product, thereby reducing the brand image of their product and causing horizontal conflict between that retailer and other retailers of the product.
Q: Give examples of businesses that may be likely to experience      (a) horizontal conflict and (b) vertical conflict.

Channel behaviour and organisation
A: Channel conflict is not always a bad thing. It can encourage the creation of well thought through policies and procedures. Conflict can eliminate inactive or high-cost channel participants particularly wholesalers and retailers who are not proactive in providing service and reducing the costs. Therefore, conflict can lead to improved service and reduced costs.
Q: Is channel conflict always a ‘bad’ thing?

A: The corporate VMS example would be Dulux Paints which are sold online to professional painters from Dulux’s own website, sold through Dulux-owned trade outlets to trade customers, and sold by retailers to end consumers. The advantage of the corporate VMS is that it gives the owner (in this case Dulux) control and by dealing with professional painters direct they can obtain market feedback on technical matters and on any changes in end-customer needs and preferences. Disadvantages - can restrict the opportunities to sell through other wholesalers and retailers. Can also create channel conflicts between their own direct sales and other retailers wishing to sell end-customers. This would typically be in the form of who “owns” the customer and what prices should be charged on particular products.
Q: A corporate VMS is a vertical marketing system that combines successive stages of production and distribution under single ownership; Q: Give an example of a company that uses a corporate VMS. What are some advantages and disadvantages of a corporate VMS?

A: Competitors may choose to join together to enter new markets where they can reach more customers and new customers and share the costs - example participating in trade expos in foreign countries, and code-sharing by airlines - which means competing airlines can code share to increase the size of their network, increase frequency of their flights and have their own customers continue to make one booking through their own booking system rather have “their” customers boo with competing airlines and therefore run the risk of familiarising their customers with services of competitors. (Code-sharing – one plane flies a route under more than one flight code; For example, code-sharing the one plan across Emirates, British airways and Qantas.)
A horizontal marketing system is a channel arrangement in which two or more companies at one level join together to follow a new marketing opportunity.
Q: Why might a company choose to join with a competitor in this form of channel arrangement? Can you give some examples?

Channel behaviour and organisation
Q: Can you think of some industries where disintermediation has occurred? What opportunities or problems might arise from disintermediation?
A: Disintermediation: travel agents have been disintermediated by airlines because customers can buy their tickets direct from the airline. Retailers have been disintermediated by online emporia such as Amazon, Alibaba and eBay. Music retailers have been disintermediated by downloading services and streaming services. Downloading services are being disintermediated by streaming services.

Coming up next
Part 4 – Retailing and Wholesaling.
